paper_title: 3DTV: A Feedforward Interpolation Network for Real-Time View Synthesis

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Real-time free-viewpoint rendering requires balancing multi-camera redundancy with the latency constraints of interactive applications. We address this challenge by combining lightweight geometry with learning and propose 3DTV, a feedforward network for real-time sparse-view interpolation. A Delaunay-based triplet selection ensures angular coverage for each target view. Building on this, we introduce a pose-aware depth module that estimates a coarse-to-fine depth pyramid, enabling efficient feature reprojection and occlusion-aware blending. Unlike methods that require scene-specific optimization, 3DTV runs feedforward without retraining, making it practical for AR/VR, telepresence, and interactive applications. Our experiments on challenging multi-view video datasets demonstrate that 3DTV consistently achieves a strong balance of quality and efficiency, outperforming recent real-time novel-view baselines. Crucially, 3DTV avoids explicit proxies, enabling robust rendering across diverse scenes. This makes it a practical solution for low-latency multi-view streaming and interactive rendering. Project Page: https://stefanmschulz.github.io/3DTV_webpage/

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Real-time streaming and rendering multi-view video content poses fundamental challenge: the raw capture produces massive amounts of data, while only small subset of views are truly necessary for synthesizing novel viewpoints. This redundancy creates tension between fidelity and efficiency, particularly in applications such as AR/VR, telepresence, and interactive video editing, where low-latency rendering is critical. While dense view data can be well exploited by recent advances in novel view synthesis (NVS), which achieve striking photorealism through neural radiance fields, Gaussian splatting, or diffusion-based generation [2, 3, 4, 5, 6], these approaches typically incur heavy computational costs, require perscene retraining, and fail to meet the demands of real-time or streaming scenarios. In practice, existing approaches trade off latency, capture density, scene complexity (e.g. multi-object environments) and processing requirements as summarized in Table 1. In light of these trade-offs, classical image-based rendering methods demonstrate that not all views are equally valuable: selecting geometrically meaningful subset can suffice for faithful interpolation. Inspired by the seminal work of Chen and Williams on view interpolation [7], we revisit this principle with modern learning-based techniques. Specifically, we propose sparse-view interpolation framework based on Delaunay triangulation of camera positions, ensuring that each novel viewpoint is synthesized from geometrically consistent triplet of source cameras. This geometric conditioning reduces redundancy and yields balanced angular coverage, addressing one of the central limitations of heuristic nearest-neighbor selection. Yet, novel view interpolation remains difficult in practice. Correspondence matching and occlusion handling are longstanding challenges, especially under wide baselines and complex scene geometry. We address these issues by adapting advances from the frame interpolation literature, replacing motioncentric flow reasoning with geometry-aware depth estimation. Our network employs coarse-to-fine pyramid architecture that progressively refines depth hypotheses across scales, enabling robust feature projection with significantly reduced computational overhead. Together, these contributions yield feedforward architecture capable of real-time novel view synthesis from only small set of input cameras, without scenespecific retraining. By combining geometric view selection, depth-guided feature projection, and efficient hierarchical refinement, our approach bridges classical principles and modern neural rendering, advancing the goal of real-time free-viewpoint video synthesis. Contributions. We present 3DTV, 3-camera Delaunay Triangulation-based View interpolation framework that combines principled geometric view selection with efficient depth-guided synthesis. Our method achieves high-quality results under real-time constraints while requiring no per-scene retraining. In summary, our contributions are as follows: Delaunay triangulation-based view selection strategy that identifies geometrically consistent camera triplets, enabling sparse-view interpolation from only three input cameras. coarse-to-fine pyramid depth estimation and fusion architecture enabling efficient geometryaware feature projection for real-time view synthesis. Extensive experiments demonstrating strong generalization to real-world data and competitive performance in both quality and runtime. Code and trained models will be publicly released upon acceptance.

sections.title: 2.1 Frame Interpolation
sections.content: Frame interpolation is the task of generating intermediate frames from surrounding frames to ensure smooth and coherent transitions within (video) sequence. Early classical approaches typically relied on explicit motion estimation as optical flow [13, 14, 15] or phase-shifts [16] to guide pixel interpolation. With the advent of data-driven techniques, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) became widely used for this task [17, 18, 19, 20, 21]. Several studies have addressed large temporal gaps or large motions between consecutive frames [22, 23, 24], e.g. by applying transformer architectures [24] or incorporating feature correlation into the loss function [23]. Other approaches focused on improving spatial awareness and reducing computational cost [25], while further research addressed the challenges such as non-uniform 3DTV: Feedforward Interpolation Network S.Schulz et al. Recently, diffusion-based approaches have emerged as powerful alternative, achieving high-fidelity results through probabilistic hierarchical sampling [28, 29, 30]. Such methods include flow-guided diffusion [28], diffusion transformers for large motions [29], and architectures specifically designed for animation videos [30]. Many of these works [14, 15, 18, 31, 23] rely on optical flow information to model temporal progression. Free-viewpoint interpolation can be interpreted as form of frame interpolation. Inspired by Reda et al. [23], we replace central optical flow with depth estimation in the target camera to account for viewpoint changes rather than temporal motion.

sections.title: 2.2 Multi-View Stereo
sections.content: Multi-view stereo (MVS) estimates dense 3D geometry from calibrated images by enforcing photoconsistency across views. Traditional methods explored volumetric [32], point-based [33], and depth map-based approaches [34, 35]. Recent learningbased approaches instead infer depth or 3D structure directly from images using learned priors. MVSNet [36] constructs cost volumes from deep features and regularizes them with 3D CNNs. To reduce memory consumption, follow-up work proposed recurrent regularization scheme over learned features [37] or coarse-to-fine cost volume approaches [38, 39, 40]. Building on the success of vision transformers in wide range of computer vision tasks [41, 42, 43], transformer-based architectures have recently been introduced to MVS, further enhancing reconstruction robustness even in textureless regions [44, 45, 46, 47, 48]. In particular, TransMVSNet [44] was the first to use attention to capture longrange intraand inter-view feature dependencies, followed by others that additionally incorporated epipolar constraints [45, 47, 49]. Robustness was improved with multi-stage attention [48], while nonLambertian surfaces were addressed using reference view synthesis loss [50]. While transformer-based MVS focuses on longrange feature evaluation during reconstruction, our method instead uses triangulation-driven view selection with lightweight, depth-oriented network to achieve real-time interpolation.

sections.title: 2.3 Novel View Synthesis
sections.content: Novel view synthesis (NVS) focuses on generating realistic images from unseen camera viewpoints. Recently, Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) [2], which represent scene with neural implicit functions, have become the dominant approach in this field. Many extensions [51, 12, 52, 53, 54] have been proposed to improve reconstruction quality, generalization, and efficiency. Follow-up works such as Instant-NGP [3] significantly accelerate training and inference, [4] introduce 3D Gaussian Splatwhile Kerbl et al. ting, which represents the scene with 3D Gaussians and further improves real-time rendering capabilities. Following Gaussian-based methods work on improvement for sparse inputs [55, 56, 57, 58, 59, 1], single input [60] and speed [59, 61, 62, 11]. In parallel, diffusion models, first developed for image generation [63, 64], have recently been applied to novel view synthesis [65, 66], from single [67, 68] or multiple views [5, 6]. related line of research targets real-time rendering/NVS without scene-specific retraining. PixelNeRF [69] and IBRNet [70] infer radiance fields directly from input views, but remain slower than real-time. FWD [71] achieves real-time rendering by combining forward warping with depth cues, while Guo et al. [72] propose category-agnostic method for real-time NVS. In the human NVS domain, GPSGaussian [11] and GPSGaussian+ [1] enable real-time view synthesis without fine-tuning, whereas SnapSnap [73] achieves high-quality reconstructions but 3 3DTV: Feedforward Interpolation Network S.Schulz et al. lacks real-time capability. height h: Our method combines principled geometric view selection with lightweight depth-guided feedforward network, achieving real-time novel view synthesis without scene-specific retraining or optimization.

sections.title: 3 Method
sections.content: We address bounded novel-view interpolation by combining geometric view selection with lightweight depth-guided network. Our pipeline first selects supporting cameras via Delaunay triangulation (Section A), ensuring balanced angular coverage for each query view. For each image, feature pyramids are extracted using an efficient network architecture (Section 3.2). From those, we regress depth and alpha for the target view in coarse to fine fashion via residual update network (Section 3.3). The fusion network combines the features through differentiable projection, providing additional information for the upper depth estimation layers in feedback loop, and synthesizes the final images (Section 3.4). Each input view Ii, {1, 2, 3} is accompanied by foreground mask Mi, which provides coarse subject segmentation. During synthesis, the network predicts the target camera image I4 as well as per-pixel latent features l, depth Dl and alpha maps Al at each pyramid level {0, . . . , 6}.

sections.title: Delaunay Triangulation
sections.content: To synthesize query viewpoint q, we select sparse set of three supporting input cameras. Standard kNearest Neighbor selection in R3 often yields poorly conditioned configurations. We instead propose projection-based strategy leveraging 2D Delaunay triangulation to ensure the query is spatially bracketed by its neighbors. For inward-facing camera setups, we fit cylinder to the camera centers {pi}, defined by axis a, base o, radius r, and height h. We perform two-stage projection: 1. Radial Normalization: Centers are projected onto the cylinder surface to remove depth bias: pcyl = + pi o, aa + vi vi , (1) where vi = (pi o) pi o, aa 2. Perspective Mapping: Points are mapped from the origin onto projection plane at ui = + o, pcyl (pcyl o) (2) We compute the Delaunay triangulation over {ui}. For any query q, we identify the enclosing triangle τ via the Muller-Trumbore ray intersection algorithm [74], ensuring the selected triplet τ provides complete angular basis for interpolation. Additional details and visualizations are provided in Section A.

sections.title: 3.2 Efficient Feature Extraction Back-
sections.content: bone To satisfy the computational constraints of real-time synthesis, we design lightweight hierarchical backbone that extracts semantically meaningful features at low channel count, thereby reducing the projection overhead described in Section 3.3. The architecture follows the efficiency principles of GhostNet [75] and GhostNetV2 [76]. Each block is built upon Ghost module, where subset of intrinsic feature maps is produced via standard convolution and the remaining channels are generated through inexpensive depthwise operations. This strategy mitigates redundancy in convolutional feature generation while preserving representational capacity. }6 For each source camera i, the backbone produces seven-level feature pyramid {F l=0, where = 0 denotes the highest spatial resolution and each subsequent level reduces the resolution by factor of two. Every stage consists of strided Ghost bottleneck for spatial downsampling followed by second bottleneck for feature refinement. Within each bottleneck, we incorporate lightweight channel attention mechanism based on global average pooling and channelwise reweighting to enhance global context modeling at negligible computational cost. Residual shortcut connections are employed whenever the spatial resolution and channel dimensionality permit, ensuring stable optimization. To compensate for the progressive loss of spatial detail induced by repeated downsampling, we append lightweight Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (LASPP) module at the deepest level. Inspired by MobileNetV3 [77], and ultimately rooted in the atrous spatial pyramid design of DeepLab [78], the module aggregates multi-scale context through 1 1 convolution branch, multiple depthwise separable atrous convolution branches with distinct dilation rates, and global average pooling branch, followed by feature 3DTV: Feedforward Interpolation Network S.Schulz et al. sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: We evaluate our method against diverse set of state-of-the-art baselines. These include offline optimization methods: Nerfacto-big [8], Splatfactobig [8], and FrugalNeRF [12]; and feed-forward (online) methods: ENeRF [10], GPS-Gaussian [11], GPS-Gaussian+ [1], and RIFTCast [9]. We report standard metrics: Peak Signal-To-Noise Ratio (PSNR), Structural Similarity (SSIM), and Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS). Our evaluation spans six benchmarks covering diverse lighting, motion, and outfits: DNA Rendering [85], LLFF [86], MVHumanNet [87], RIFTCast [9], THuman2.1 [88], and ZJUMoCap [89]. Notably, LLFF is included as an out-of-distribution stress test to evaluate robustness on forward-facing real-world scenes with significantly larger depth ranges. Methods requiring per-scene optimization (hereafter denoted with *) are evaluated on representative subset. Unless stated otherwise, experiments are performed at resolution of 10242. sections.title: 3.6 Implementation Details
sections.content: We train on synthetic dataset comprising 24,753 samples generated from 357 3D assets [82, 83, 84] and additional curated Blender scenes. Each sample contains three source views and target view, which is randomly sampled via barycentric coordinates with an additional 20cm forward-axis jitter to ensure robustness. The model is trained end-to-end on single NVIDIA A40 and we target the NVIDIA RTX 4090 for inference, where our hardware-efficient profile enables real-time performance. Optimization details, augmentation scheme, and loss weights are provided in Section B. 7 Fig. 4 compares our method against recent feedforward and real-time capable baselines. Across human captures and general scenes, 2-view approaches such as GPS-Gaussian/ + frequently suffer from depth ambiguity under wide baselines, leading to floating structures, duplicated geometry, and temporal instability. By conditioning synthesis on geometrically consistent 3-camera triplet and performing depth-guided feature projection, our method produces more stable geometry and preserves fine details such as faces and extremities more reliably than 2view systems. In contrast to RIFTCast, which relies on processing all available views and full-view mask sets to construct visual hull, our approach maintains consistent results using only the selected triplet. Quantitative results in Table 2 highlight the qualityefficiency trade-off. While per-scene optimization methods (e.g., Splatfacto-big, Nerfacto-big) achieve the strongest metrics, their multi-minute reconstruction overhead makes them impractical for interactive settings. Among sparse-view methods using only 23 inputs, our approach consistently achieves competitive or superior quality compared to prior feed-forward baselines on human-centric datasets (e.g., MVHumanNet and ZJUMoCap), indicating robustness to imperfect foreground masks and real capture artifacts. sections.title: 4.2 Runtime and Memory Analysis
sections.content: In Table 3, we analyze the computational demands of the available approaches. Optimization-based methods (Nerfacto, Splatfacto) provide superior results but fail to meet the requirements of real-time applications due to their heavy per-scene training requirements. Our method, particularly when optimized via TensorRT (OursRT), demonstrates significant advantage in resource-constrained environments. At 1024 1024 resolution, our optimized model achieves 40 FPS with peak memory footprint of only 2.2 GB, outperforming GPS-Gaussian and RIFTCast in terms of the quality-to-memory ratio. While several recent methods report real-time inference through highly optimized deployment pipelines, these implementations are often not publicly available. In our work, we aim to ensure reproducibility by providing the full TensorRT deployment configuration alongside the model. Combined with our lightweight architecture and hierarchical feature projection, this enables transparent and practical path to real-time performance.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: While our Ghost-based backbone is efficient, achieving true real-time performance (> 60 FPS) on would require further optimization such as weight quantization. Furthermore, the model is currently constrained to three-view inputs and indoor capture stages with limited applicability to large scale scenes. This limitation is reflected in our LLFF stress test, where large scene scales exceed the training distribution. Additionally, our method targets bounded interpolation around the selected camera triplet, handling large extrapolation beyond the camera hull remains future work. Finally, blurriness in complex regions suggests that increasing feature map density or adopting temporal feature propagation could better resolve high-frequency details and disocclusions. We introduced 3DTV, feedforward framework for real-time sparse-view novel view synthesis that combines principled geometric view selection with efficient depth-guided fusion. By leveraging Delaunay triangulation to select geometrically consistent camera triplets and estimating target-view depth through coarse-to-fine pyramid, our method enables stable wide-baseline interpolation from only three input cameras without per-scene optimization. Extensive experiments across multiple multi-view benchmarks demonstrate that 3DTV achieves strong balance between synthesis quality and computational efficiency, outperforming recent real-time sparse-view baselines while maintaining low latency. Despite being trained solely on synthetic data, the model generalizes robustly to real-world captures and diverse scene configurations. These results suggest that combining lightweight geometric reasoning with 10 3DTV: Feedforward Interpolation Network S.Schulz et al. (a) Depth Map generated by our method. (b) Generalization capabilities of our model to varying depth of the camera. (c) Generalization capabilities of our model to higher resolutions such as 2048 2048. (d) failure case where two persons behind one another get washed out. Figure 5: Generalization behavior of 3DTV across geometry, viewpoint, and resolution. Despite syntheticonly training, the model predicts coherent depth and stable synthesis under varying camera depths and scales to higher output resolutions without retraining. feedforward neural synthesis is promising direction for scalable real-time free-viewpoint video rendering. sections.title: Supplementary
sections.content: This appendix provides additional technical details, expanded experimental results, and comprehensive visual comparisons to support the claims presented in the main paper. Specifically, this supplemental material includes: Technical Implementation: Detailed derivations regarding the Delaunay Triangulation used for mesh generation, along with deep dive into the network architecture. Training Protocols: Comprehensive details on the training regime, including our choice of optimizer, specific loss function weightings, training duration, and the data augmentation strategies employed. Dataset Characteristics: breakdown of the datasets used, including source resolutions and the specific preprocessing pipeline applied. Extended Baseline Comparisons: Evaluations against two additional baseline models. We provide their results here for completeness and to justify their exclusion from the main manuscript. Qualitative & Quantitative Analysis: Expanded visual results comparing our method against the primary baselines and across various ablation studies. Delaunay Triangulation Ablation As described in Sec. 3.1 of the main paper, the triangulation process for camera setups in capture stage is visualized in Fig. 6. First, cylinder is fitted to the camera positions, and all positions are projected onto its surface. ray is then cast from an Origin point, defined as the center of the elongated base (visualized here as shifted origin for better clarity), through each camera location and onto 2D plane positioned parallel to the cylinders top. Standard Delaunay triangulation is computed on this 2D plane, and the resulting faces are backprojected into 3D. These faces are subsequently used to identify the corresponding triangle of source cameras for novel view. This triangulation approach offers several advantages: It avoids rendering images from the floor while naturally supporting top-down views, which remain consistently covered. The method generalizes easily to other geometries, such as cuboid setups, by simply fitting cuboid instead of cylinder while keeping the remaining pipeline intact. We evaluate the hyperparameters of this algorithm, specifically the elongation (offset) of the Origin point and the placement of the 2D projection plane. Based on the results in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8, we find that positioning the Origin 1m below the cylinder and the projection plane 1m above the cylinder yields the optimal triangulation for capture stages. Increasing the distance of the Origin or the projection plane excessively leads to artifacts: triangles at the bottom are frequently clipped, and the resulting mesh tends to follow vertical lines. This creates thin, elongated triangles rather than the near-equilateral triangles that are preferable for stable interpolation.

sections.title: B Training Details
sections.content: B.1 Dataset Curation As our network requires ground truth depth maps for stable training and faster convergence, we could not use any real-world dataset for this task. Thus, we train on synthetic dataset comprising 24,753 samples generated from 357 3D assets [82, 83, 84] and additional curated Blender scenes. We downloaded various blender scenes and material maps from polyhaven [82] and sketchfab [83] ranging from coaches to chemistry workbenches and scaled the scenes to fit in volume of [2m, 2m] [2m, 2m] [0m, 2m] which approximately matches standard capture stage volumes. Additionally, we used the HumanGenerator3D add-on [84] for Blender to render humans in different poses and clothing styles. Most of the scenes contain only one (complex) object so decided to add custom scenes. These scenes are mad up of randomly placed, scaled, and deformed cubes with different images covering the surfaces of each cube. This was used to help the model resolve ambiguous depth estimations for neighboring pixels. To cover wide range of illumination patterns and real world lighting we used environment maps from polyhaven [82] in Blender. As final step, random camera positions and random camera amounts with different depth offsets along the approximative cylinder covering the volume are created. To render novel views, we apply the triangulation discussed in Sec. 3.1 in the main paper and place cameras based on randomly chosen barycentric weights. The orientation is adjusted accordingly to maintain proper rotation matrix. With all cameras 17 3DTV: Feedforward Interpolation Network S.Schulz et al. sections.title: Training
sections.content: The 3DTV network is optimized using the Adam optimizer with an initial learning rate of 105. We employ OneCycleLR scheduler with maximum learning rate of 103, using linear annealing strategy and final division factor of 50. Training is performed in two-stage resolutionprogressive manner to balance high-frequency detail with global structure: Phase 1 (Base): 100 epochs at 512512 resolution. This phase required approximately 4 days of compute on single NVIDIA A40 GPU with batch size of 2. Phase 2 (Fine-tuning): 25 epochs at 1024 1024 resolution to refine high-fidelity textures and interpolation boundaries. This phase required about 36 more hours under the same conditions. The total training objective is multi-task loss formulated as: Ltot = λ1L1 + λ2LRGB + λ3Ldepth + λ4Loffset + λ5Lα + λ6Lvgg + λ7Lstyle (15) The weighting coefficients λi are adjusted throughout the training phases to prioritize structural convergence initially, followed by perceptual refinement. The specific schedule is detailed in Table 5. B.3 Data Augmentation Pipeline To ensure robustness against varied capture conditions and sensor noise, we implement custom augmentation suite that helps us bridge perfect synthetic training and noisy real-world data conditions. Let be the input image and the corresponding depth map: 1. Photometric Jitter: Standard color jittering is applied to brightness (0.3), contrast (0.3), saturation (0.2), and hue (0.2). 2. Exposure and Gamma: With 30% probability, we apply Iout = γ e, where γ U(0.7, 1.3) and U(0.8, 1.0). 3. Depth-Conditioned Background Noise: Since the renderings all contain black backgrounds and we dont want the model to overfit 18 3DTV: Feedforward Interpolation Network S.Schulz et al. Post-Processing Simulation: To mimic realworld capture artifacts, we apply 3x3 Gaussian blur (σ [0.01, 0.5]) with 50% probability. Additionally, we simulate quantization/banding with 30% probability by discretizing the color space into {128, 192, 255} levels. 5. Stochastic Scaling: Random scaling and cropping are applied (20% chance). For 10242 inputs, the scale factor U(0.5, 0.7), which is halved for 5122 inputs. B.4 Deployment and Inference Optimization For real-time performance, the model is deployed using NVIDIA TensorRT. The PyTorch weights are first exported to an ONNX intermediate representation. We then utilize the trtexec tool to compile Bfloat16 (BF16) optimized engine. The compilation uses the --best flag to enable exhaustive search of the CUDA kernel space for the target hardware. Throughput benchmarks are reported as the average over 100 inference cycles with warm up phase beforehand of 10 cycles. To prevent decrease in model performance due to the reduction in precision, the model is trained already in Bfloat16. Dataset and Evaluation Details To ensure fair comparison across all methods, all evaluations are performed at standardized resolution of 1024 1024 pixels. For datasets with higher native resolutions, we apply bilinear downsampling. To maintain geometric consistency, the camera intrinsic matrices are scaled by the corresponding ratios in both the and dimensions. C.1 RIFTCast [9] The RIFTCast dataset contains 32 dynamic scenes captured via 35-camera multi-view rig. The sequences feature complex multi-actor interactions (e.g., playing drums) and vary from 2 to 20 seconds at 25 FPS. Data Usage: We utilize the provided RGB images, binary masks, and background matting. For real-time methods, we evaluate across all 34 available sequences. Evaluation Split: One camera is reserved strictly for evaluation. For offline baseline comparison, we restrict training to the first set of images across the first 10 scenes. C.2 DNA Rendering [85] DNA Rendering is large-scale, high-fidelity dataset for human performance rendering. We follow the selection protocol of RIFTCast [9], specifically utilizing the first 67 scenes from Part 2. Preprocessing: Original 2448 2048 images are downscaled to 1024 1024. Split: We use the 5-megapixel camera subset at frame 80, excluding cameras 3, 8, 19, and 40. Offline methods are trained on the first 10 scenes using all available views except the test cameras. 19 3DTV: Feedforward Interpolation Network S.Schulz et al. C.3 LLFF (Local Light Field Fusion) [86] The LLFF dataset consists of forward-facing scenes with varying scales. We utilize the standard scenes: fern, flower, horns, leaves, orchids, and room. Configuration: To optimize performance for 2-view and 3-view real-time setups, we selected camera poses on single horizontal level rather than the original 3-view split. Cleaning: We explicitly removed blurry frames that negatively impact offline optimization, specifically images 4030 and 4037 from fern and 2971 from flower. C.4 MVHuman [87] MVHuman provides high-resolution captures of human subjects in various attire. We utilize Part 1 of the dataset, downscaling the 1500 2048 images to 1024 1024. Data Usage: We evaluate on sequences 100001 through 100010. View Setup: Cameras A055 and A005 are used for 2-view experiments, with A037 added for the 3-view setup. Camera A029 serves as the test view. C.5 THuman2.1 [88] THuman2.1 consists of high-quality 3D human scans. We rendered these models using random stage setup with virtual camera extrinsics mimicking the Bonn Telebench rig. Preprocessing: Rendered at 2664 2304 and downscaled to 1024 1024. Selection: We take the first 500 objects for general evaluation. For offline methods, we limit the comparison to the first 10 examples to maintain computational feasibility. C.6 ZJUMoCap [89] We employ the updated ZJUMoCap dataset, using sequences 313, 315, 377, 386, 387, 390, 392, and 393. Setup: Images are natively 1024 1024. For 2-view setups, we use Cameras 1 and 5. For the 3-view setup, Camera 4 is included. Camera 3 is the held-out test view. Temporal Constraint: To ensure consistency across baselines, we limit the evaluation to the first 500 frames of each sequence. Comparison Method Failures During our evaluation of state-of-the-art baselines, we explored several additional methods that were ultimately excluded from the main paper. In this section, we analyze the technical challenges and failure modes encountered with these methods, justifying their omission from our final quantitative comparison. D.1 Snap-Snap [73] Snap-Snap is follow-up to GPS-Gaussian [11] designed to reconstruct human 3D Gaussians in milliseconds using only front and back view as input. We applied this approach to both the RIFTCast and DNA Rendering datasets; however, the results exhibited significant geometric artifacts. Specifically, we observed persistent ghosting and duplication of the subject, even when rendering from the original input views (see Fig. 9 and Fig. 10). To mitigate this, we experimented with various camera configurations, attempting to match the required opposing-view setup as closely as possible within the constraints of real-world capture stages (where perfect centering and alignment are often infeasible). Despite these efforts, the artifacts remained consistent across diverse scenes and subjects. Due to these reproducibility issues and the subsequent limited availability of the official pre-trained weights, we opted to exclude Snap-Snap from the main comparison to ensure fair and stable baseline evaluation. D.2 FWD [71] FWD is feedforward method that supports an arbitrary number of input views, making it methodologically relevant to our proposed approach. However, in our tests using 3-view configuration on the provided benchmark data, FWD struggled with geometric fidelity. As shown in Fig. 11, the method introduces noticeable aspect-ratio distortion (i.e., squeezing the object) and produces overall blurry results. Furthermore, FWD is optimized for native resolution of 640 480 pixels. Adapting the model to our 1024 1024 benchmark would have necessitated 3DTV: Feedforward Interpolation Network S.Schulz et al.
